Home

Cancellation in Rust async

Rust futures are cancelled by dropping them. Unlike Go or Java, there is no signal sent to the task — the executor simply stops polling and runs destructors. This shapes the entire programming model.

Cancel points

A future only observes cancellation at .await boundaries. Between await points, the future runs synchronously and cannot be interrupted. This is sometimes called "cancellation safety": code between awaits is effectively atomic with respect to cancel.

Drop guards

If you hold a lock, a database transaction, or a file handle across an await, the corresponding RAII guard will run on cancel. This is the language's main guarantee: resources are released. Whether your application invariants survive depends on whether the partial work is safe to roll back.

The tokio::select! gotcha

Inside tokio::select!, the unselected branches are dropped at the await point. If a future has stashed state that it needs to drive forward later — for example a buffered read in the middle of decoding a frame — dropping it loses that state. Cancellation-safe primitives (like tokio::sync::mpsc::Receiver::recv) document this explicitly. Treat undocumented futures as cancel-unsafe.

Structured concurrency

For long-running work, prefer spawning a task and holding the JoinHandle. Cancel by sending a shutdown signal through a channel; let the task drain and exit cleanly. The downside is more code; the upside is that cleanup is explicit rather than implicit-in-drop.

Example engineering notes.
